packs.name: Core Git
packs.description: Protects against destructive git commands that can lose uncommitted work, rewrite history, or destroy stashes

destructive_patterns.name: checkout-discard
destructive_patterns.reason: git checkout -- discards uncommitted changes permanently. Use 'git stash' first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git checkout -- <path> discards all uncommitted changes to the specified files in your working directory. These changes are permanently lost - they cannot be recovered because they were never committed.

Safer alternatives:
- git stash: Save changes temporarily, restore later with 'git stash pop'
- git diff <path>: Review what would be lost before discarding

Preview changes first:
  git diff -- <path>

suggestions.description: Save changes temporarily, restore later with 'git stash pop'
Review what would be lost before discarding

destructive_patterns.name: checkout-ref-discard
destructive_patterns.reason: git checkout <ref> -- <path> overwrites working tree. Use 'git stash' first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git checkout <ref> -- <path> replaces your working tree files with versions from another commit or branch. Any uncommitted changes to those files are permanently lost - they cannot be recovered.

Safer alternatives:
- git stash: Save changes first, then checkout, then restore with 'git stash pop'
- git show <ref>:<path>: View the file content without overwriting

Preview what would change:
  git diff HEAD <ref> -- <path>

suggestions.description: Save changes first, then checkout, then restore with 'git stash pop'
View the file content without overwriting
Preview what would change before overwriting

destructive_patterns.name: restore-worktree
destructive_patterns.reason: Blanket git restore (e.g., 'git restore .') discards all uncommitted changes. Specific file restores like 'git restore src/main.go' are allowed.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git restore without a specific file path (e.g., 'git restore .' or bare 'git restore') discards ALL uncommitted changes in your working directory, reverting files to their last committed state. Changes that were never committed are permanently lost.

Note: Restoring a specific file (e.g., 'git restore src/main.go') is allowed because it targets only one file and is a common, intentional operation.

Safer alternatives:
- git restore --staged <path>: Only unstage, keeps working directory changes
- git stash: Save all changes temporarily
- git diff <path>: Review what would be lost

Preview changes first:
  git diff <path>

suggestions.description: Only unstage, keeps working directory changes intact
Save all changes temporarily, restore later with 'git stash pop'
Review what would be lost before discarding

destructive_patterns.name: restore-worktree-explicit
destructive_patterns.reason: git restore --worktree/-W discards uncommitted changes permanently.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git restore --worktree (or -W) explicitly targets your working directory, discarding uncommitted changes. Even when combined with --staged, the worktree changes are permanently lost.

Safer alternatives:
- git restore --staged <path>: Only unstage, keeps working directory
- git stash: Save changes first

Preview changes first:
  git diff <path>

suggestions.description: Only unstage, keeps working directory changes intact
Save all changes temporarily before discarding
Review what would be lost before discarding

destructive_patterns.name: reset-hard
destructive_patterns.reason: git reset --hard destroys uncommitted changes. Use 'git stash' first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git reset --hard discards ALL uncommitted changes in your working directory AND staging area. This is one of the most dangerous git commands because changes that were never committed cannot be recovered by any means.

What gets destroyed:
- All modified files revert to the target commit
- All staged changes are lost
- Untracked files remain (use git clean to remove those)

Safer alternatives:
- git reset --soft <ref>: Move HEAD but keep all changes staged
- git reset --mixed <ref>: Move HEAD, unstage changes, keep working dir (default)
- git stash: Save changes before resetting

Preview what would be lost:
  git status && git diff

suggestions.description: Save all uncommitted changes before reset
Undo commit but keep all changes staged
Undo commit, unstage changes, but keep working directory
Reset a specific file only, preserving other changes

destructive_patterns.name: reset-merge
destructive_patterns.reason: git reset --merge can lose uncommitted changes.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git reset --merge resets the index and updates files in the working tree that differ between the target commit and HEAD, but keeps changes that are not staged. However, if there are uncommitted changes in files that need to be updated, those changes will be lost.

Safer alternatives:
- git stash: Save uncommitted changes before reset
- git merge --abort: If in the middle of a merge, abort safely

Preview what would change:
  git status && git diff

suggestions.description: Save uncommitted changes before reset
Abort the current merge safely without losing changes
Preview what would change before resetting

destructive_patterns.name: clean-force
destructive_patterns.reason: git clean -f/--force removes untracked files permanently. Review with 'git clean -n' first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git clean -f permanently deletes untracked files from your working directory. These are files that have never been committed to git, so they cannot be recovered from git history. If you haven't backed them up elsewhere, they are gone forever.

Common dangerous combinations:
- git clean -fd: Also removes untracked directories
- git clean -xf: Also removes ignored files (build artifacts, .env, etc.)

Safer alternatives:
- git clean -n: Dry-run, shows what would be deleted
- git clean -i: Interactive mode, choose what to delete

ALWAYS preview first:
  git clean -n -d

suggestions.description: Dry run first (shows what would be deleted)
Dry run including directories
Interactive mode, choose what to delete
Stash instead of delete (recoverable)

destructive_patterns.name: push-force-long
destructive_patterns.reason: Force push can destroy remote history. Use --force-with-lease if necessary.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git push --force overwrites remote history with your local history. This can permanently destroy commits that others have already pulled, causing data loss for your entire team. Collaborators may lose work, and recovering requires manual intervention from everyone affected.

What can go wrong:
- Commits others pushed are deleted from remote
- Team members get diverged histories
- CI/CD pipelines may reference deleted commits

Safer alternative:
- git push --force-with-lease: Only forces if remote matches your last fetch

Check remote state first:
  git fetch && git log origin/<branch>..HEAD

suggestions.description: Fails if remote has new commits you haven't fetched
Even safer: also checks that your local ref includes the remote ref
Preview what you're about to overwrite on the remote

destructive_patterns.name: push-force-short
destructive_patterns.reason: Force push (-f) can destroy remote history. Use --force-with-lease if necessary.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git push -f (short for --force) overwrites remote history with your local history. This can permanently destroy commits that others have already pulled, causing data loss for your entire team.

What can go wrong:
- Commits others pushed are deleted from remote
- Team members get diverged histories
- CI/CD pipelines may reference deleted commits

Safer alternative:
- git push --force-with-lease: Only forces if remote matches your last fetch

Check remote state first:
  git fetch && git log origin/<branch>..HEAD

suggestions.description: Fails if remote has new commits you haven't fetched
Even safer: also checks that your local ref includes the remote ref
Preview what you're about to overwrite on the remote

destructive_patterns.name: push-mirror
destructive_patterns.reason: git push --mirror overwrites the entire remote. Use 'git push' for specific branches.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git push --mirror forces the remote repository to match your local repository exactly. This means:

- Remote branches not in your local repo are DELETED
- Remote tags not in your local repo are DELETED
- All remote refs are overwritten
- Other contributors' work on remote-only branches is destroyed

This is rarely what you want. It's intended for creating exact mirrors of repositories.

Safer alternatives:
- git push origin <branch>: Push specific branch
- git push --all: Push all local branches (without deleting remote-only ones)
- git push --tags: Push all tags

suggestions.description: Push a specific branch instead of mirroring everything
Push all local branches without deleting remote-only branches
Push all tags to remote
Review remote state before pushing

destructive_patterns.name: branch-force-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: git branch -D/--force deletes branches without checks. Recoverable via 'git reflog'.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git branch -D force-deletes a branch without checking if it has been merged. If the branch contains unmerged commits, you may lose access to that work. However, the commits still exist in git's object database and can be recovered using reflog (for a limited time, typically 90 days).

Safer alternatives:
- git branch -d <branch>: Safe delete, fails if branch is not fully merged
- Merge the branch first, then delete with -d

Recovery if needed:
  git reflog  # Find the commit hash
  git checkout -b <branch> <commit-hash>

suggestions.description: Safe delete: only works if branch is fully merged
Show branch info (last commit) before deleting
Review branch commits before deleting

destructive_patterns.name: stash-drop
destructive_patterns.reason: git stash drop deletes a single stash. Recoverable via `git fsck` (unreachable objects).
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git stash drop removes a specific stash entry from your stash list. The stashed changes become unreferenced but remain in git's object database temporarily. They can often be recovered using git fsck, but this is not guaranteed and becomes harder over time as git garbage collects.

Safer alternatives:
- git stash pop: Apply and drop in one step (only drops if apply succeeds)
- git stash apply: Apply without dropping, verify first

Recovery if needed:
  git fsck --unreachable | grep commit
  git show <commit-hash>  # Inspect each to find your stash

suggestions.description: Apply and drop atomically (only drops if apply succeeds)
Apply without dropping, verify changes first
Preview stash contents before dropping
Review all stashes before dropping any

destructive_patterns.name: stash-clear
destructive_patterns.reason: git stash clear permanently deletes ALL stashed changes.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
git stash clear removes ALL stash entries at once. Unlike git stash drop, which removes one at a time, this command wipes your entire stash list. All stashed changes become unreferenced and are very difficult to recover.

What gets destroyed:
- All entries in 'git stash list' are removed
- Multiple sets of saved work-in-progress may be lost

Safer alternatives:
- git stash drop stash@{n}: Remove one specific stash at a time
- git stash list: Review what would be lost first
- git stash show stash@{n}: Inspect each stash before deciding

Recovery (difficult, not guaranteed):
  git fsck --unreachable | grep commit

suggestions.description: Remove one specific stash at a time
Review all stashes before clearing
Inspect each stash before deciding to delete

packs.name: Core Filesystem
packs.description: Protects against dangerous rm -rf commands outside temp directories

destructive_patterns.name: rm-rf-root-home
destructive_patterns.reason: rm -rf on root or home paths is EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. This command will NOT be executed. Ask the user to run it manually if truly needed.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
This command would recursively delete files starting from the root filesystem (/) or home directory (~). This is catastrophic and will destroy:

- Your entire operating system
- All installed applications and libraries
- All user data, documents, and configurations
- Boot files, making the system unbootable

There is NO recovery without backups. Even with backups, full restoration takes hours to days.

If you need to delete specific files, use explicit paths:
  rm -rf /path/to/specific/directory

Always preview what would be deleted first:
  find /path/to/directory -type f | head -20

suggestions.description: Preview what files would be deleted before running
List directory contents to verify the path
Use explicit, specific paths instead of root or home

destructive_patterns.name: rm-rf-general
destructive_patterns.reason: rm -rf is destructive and requires human approval. Explain what you want to delete and why, then ask the user to run the command manually.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
rm -rf recursively removes files and directories without confirmation prompts. The -f (force) flag suppresses all warnings, making accidental deletions silent and immediate.

Why this is dangerous:
- Deleted files bypass the trash - they're gone immediately
- Typos in paths can delete unintended directories
- Wildcards can expand to match more than expected
- No undo mechanism exists

Safe alternatives:
- rm -ri: Interactive mode, confirms each file
- trash-cli: Moves files to trash instead of deleting
- rm -rf in /tmp, /var/tmp, $TMPDIR: Allowed (safe temp directories)

Preview what would be deleted:
  find /path/to/delete -type f | wc -l  # Count files
  ls -la /path/to/delete               # List contents

suggestions.description: Interactive mode: confirms each file before deletion
Move to trash instead of permanent deletion (requires trash-cli)
Move to trash via GNOME (requires gio)
Move to a temp holding area instead of deleting immediately
Safe temp directory deletion (allowed without confirmation)
Count files that would be deleted before proceeding
List directory contents to verify the path

destructive_patterns.name: rm-r-f-separate
destructive_patterns.reason: rm with separate -r -f flags is destructive and requires human approval.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
rm with separate -r and -f flags has the same effect as rm -rf: recursive forced deletion without confirmation.

Common variations that are all equivalent:
- rm -r -f path
- rm -f -r path
- rm -r -f -v path (verbose but still forced)

All carry the same risks as rm -rf: immediate, silent, irreversible deletion.

Safer approach for temporary directories:
- rm -r -f /tmp/mydir    # Allowed - temp directories are safe
- rm -r -f $TMPDIR/mydir # Allowed - uses system temp dir

For other paths, prefer:
  rm -ri /path  # Interactive confirmation

suggestions.description: Interactive mode: confirms each file before deletion
Safe temp directory deletion (allowed without confirmation)
Use system temp directory (allowed without confirmation)
Preview files before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: rm-recursive-force-long
destructive_patterns.reason: rm --recursive --force is destructive and requires human approval.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
rm --recursive --force is the long-form equivalent of rm -rf. While more readable, it carries identical risks: silent, recursive, irreversible deletion.

The long flags may appear in:
- Scripts aiming for clarity
- Generated code from build tools
- Cross-platform compatibility scenarios

All standard rm -rf precautions apply:
- Verify the path before running
- Use absolute paths to avoid ambiguity
- Consider using trash-cli for recoverable deletion

Preview command:
  find /path --maxdepth 2 -ls | head -30

suggestions.description: Interactive mode: confirms each file before deletion
Preview directory structure before deletion
Safe temp directory deletion (allowed without confirmation)

destructive_patterns.name: rm-recursive-no-force
destructive_patterns.reason: rm -r/--recursive deletes directories recursively. Without -f, prompts for write-protected files.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
rm -r (or -R/--recursive) without -f recursively deletes directories and their contents. This is less dangerous than rm -rf because without the -f flag, the command will prompt for confirmation on write-protected files, giving the user a chance to abort.

However, it is still destructive:
- All files and subdirectories are deleted
- Deleted files bypass the trash
- Typos in paths can delete the wrong directory
- Non-write-protected files are deleted without prompting

Safer alternatives:
- ls -la <path>: Preview contents before deletion
- rm -ri <path>: Interactive mode, confirms each file
- mv <path> /tmp/: Move instead of delete

suggestions.description: Preview directory contents before deletion
Interactive mode: confirms each file before deletion
Move to temp instead of deleting permanently
Count files that would be deleted

packs.name: Core TW
packs.description: Prevents agents from disabling TW protections via tw allow or direct config file writes

destructive_patterns.name: tw-allow
destructive_patterns.reason: tw allow modifies TW's safety rules. Suggest the command to the user to run manually.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
tw allow adds exceptions to TW's protection rules, which can disable safety checks for destructive commands. An agent should never weaken its own safety guardrails.

The user can run tw allow commands directly in their terminal (outside the hook) if they choose to.

Read-only operations are safe:
  tw allow --list

suggestions.description: List current allow rules (read-only, safe)
Check if a command would be denied without modifying rules

destructive_patterns.name: tw-overrides-write
destructive_patterns.reason: Direct writes to .tw/overrides.json bypass tw allow. Suggest the change to the user.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Writing directly to .tw/overrides.json modifies TW's override configuration, which could disable safety protections. This has the same effect as running tw allow commands.

The user can edit this file manually if they choose to.

suggestions.description: View current overrides (read-only, safe)

destructive_patterns.name: tw-allowkey-write
destructive_patterns.reason: Direct writes to .tw/allow.key bypass tw allow. Suggest the change to the user.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Writing directly to .tw/allow.key modifies TW's allow key, which could be used to bypass safety protections.

The user can edit this file manually if they choose to.

suggestions.description: View current allow key (read-only, safe)
